title: Full-Path Critique Framework for Signed-Language Writing and Text Infrastructure
subtitle: A methodological guide for evaluating writing systems, notation systems, encodings, standards, tools, datasets, research proposals, and infrastructure claims
summary: A reusable evaluation framework for signed-language writing systems, text infrastructure, encodings, standards, tools, datasets, and adjacent-purpose systems.
abstract: This framework provides a reusable method for evaluating signed-language writing systems, encodings, standards, tools, datasets, and representation proposals at the layer they actually address and against the problem they actually claim to solve. It separates human writing, written convention, written units, spatial and compositional organization, technical text models, encoding, text operations, layout, rendering, tooling, literacy practice, governance, and stewardship before judging claims. It also treats notation, transcription, semantic representation, pedagogical bridge systems, corpus annotation, video records, character encodings, renderers, dictionary platforms, and AI datasets as adjacent-purpose systems that may be valuable without satisfying full-path signed-text infrastructure requirements for signed-language literacy as durable written text. The framework preserves useful layer-level insights while identifying full-path overextension, evidence mismatch, authority conflation, data-object collapse, and unmet infrastructure requirements.
synopsis: The shared evaluative framework distinguishes system layers from adjacent-purpose categories and evaluation axes. It gives a reusable method for evaluating papers, theses, proposals, tools, datasets, and standards without treating narrow usefulness as full-path adequacy for signed-language literacy as durable written text or collapsing technical, human, evidentiary, data-governance, and stewardship questions into one opinion.

slides.title: Why A Framework
slides.summary: Signed-language writing proposals are often misread when system layers, adjacent purposes, evidence, authority, and infrastructure are collapsed.
slides.speech: This framework exists because signed-language writing proposals often mix layers and purposes. A source may be useful as notation, weak as writing, strong as encoding, or incomplete as infrastructure. The framework separates those questions before judging the work.

image_prompts.concrete: Methodology slide showing signed-language writing claims separated into system layers, adjacent representational targets, evidence axes, and infrastructure requirements
image_prompts.abstract: Overlapping claims sorted into clear transparent layers

slides.title: Threshold Distinctions
slides.summary: The method depends on separating writing from encoding and rendering, symbols from written signs, serialization from linear writing, adjacent purposes from full-path infrastructure, and data objects from rights layers.
bullets: Writing system, encoding, and rendering differ
A symbol is not a written sign
Data objects and rights layers differ
slides.speech: The threshold distinctions make the framework necessary. Writing, encoding, and rendering are not the same. A symbol is not the same as a written sign. Serialization is not evidence that writing is linear. Adjacent-purpose systems are not failed writing systems. Written signs, lexical records, corpora, datasets, media, rights, and consent are also different objects.
slides.notes: This is the core teaching slide.

image_prompts.concrete: Five-part academic slide separating writing from encoding, symbols from written signs, serialization from linear writing, adjacent-purpose systems from full-path writing infrastructure, and data objects from rights layers
image_prompts.abstract: Five precise distinctions opening a clean review framework

slides.title: Evaluation Layers
slides.summary: The framework evaluates system layers, adjacent-purpose categories, claim scope, evidence status, maturity, authority type, audience, completeness, interdependence, and prior-work engagement.
bullets: Compare claims with evidence and authority
slides.speech: The framework asks recurring questions. What layer is the source solving? Is it a full-path writing claim or an adjacent-purpose claim? What is the primary written unit? How does it treat space, text operations, evidence, maturity, authority, audience, prior work, and stewardship?
slides.notes: Keep the list usable, not exhaustive.

image_prompts.concrete: Layered evaluation checklist for signed-language writing systems, with rows for written convention, written units, text model, encoding, operations, layout, rendering, tooling, practice, governance, and evidence status
image_prompts.abstract: A vertical stack of review layers forming a reusable analytic instrument

slides.title: Output Classification
slides.summary: The framework separates layer-level judgment from full-path judgment, then records what would be needed next.
slides.speech: The framework also needs a way to classify outputs. Some claims are correct and useful. Some are useful but narrow. Some are strong partial paths. Some become misleading when a real layer-level success is overextended into full-path adequacy. The classification keeps critique from becoming all-or-nothing and asks what would be needed next.
slides.notes: This slide makes the method more usable. It gives readers a vocabulary for judgment without flattening every reviewed source into right or wrong.

image_prompts.concrete: Review classification slide with layer-level judgment, full-path judgment, useful but narrow contributions, overextended claims, and fundamentally misframed claims shown as a scholarly decision tree
image_prompts.abstract: A careful evaluation rubric sorting claims by usefulness and fit

slides.title: Final Principle
slides.summary: The goal is constructive critique: preserve what each source solves while refusing to let one layer become the whole answer.
bullets: Use critique to build better infrastructure
slides.speech: The final principle is constructive. The review should preserve what each source actually solves while refusing to let one layer become the whole answer. That is how critique becomes useful for future writing systems, tools, standards, datasets, and research.

image_prompts.concrete: Constructive critique slide showing useful source insights preserved while overextended claims are bounded by layer-level and full-path judgment
image_prompts.abstract: A review frame preserving valid insights while preventing one layer from becoming the whole answer
